title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who learned the craft of bookbinding and restoration. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.C:
### Marco, The Archivist’s Apprentice
'I’ve always loved the smell of old paper,' Marco says. [51]The most difficult part was the painstaking process of de-acidifying every single page of a 17th-century ledger.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my manual dexterity and steady hands improved, which helped me in my hobby of building model ships.[/48] [53]I learned that a book is a physical vessel for history, and my job is to be an invisible caretaker.[/53] [50]I realized that my background in chemistry was essential for understanding the various glue reactions and paper aging.[/50] [47]Initially, the extreme silence and isolation of the archive were quite a shock to my extroverted personality.[/47] It was a profound exercise in patience.
texts.D:
### Sarah, The Artistic Binder
Sarah creates modern bindings for classic texts. [49]The experience taught me that a cover shouldn't just protect a book, but should be an artistic interpretation of its contents.[/49] [52]I hadn't anticipated the deep emotional connection I’d feel to a client’s childhood diary that I was tasked with rebinding.[/52] [47]I was quite startled by how much the repetitive sewing of sections affected my eyesight in the first few months.[/47] [54]What I valued most was the wisdom of my mentor, who taught me that 'good enough' is never an option in restoration.[/54] [56]If I hadn't taken a short taster course at a craft fair, I never would have pursued it professionally.[/56] It was a creative turning point.
texts.A:
### Victor, The Self-Taught Collector
Victor restores books he finds in flea markets. [51]I struggled with the scarcity of authentic materials; finding the right type of goat leather or linen thread was a constant battle.[/51] [48]I noticed that my general ability to focus for hours without distraction reached an all-time high, which was a great mental benefit.[/48] [53]I realized that restoration is not about making something look new, but about making it stable for another century.[/53] [50]I found that the meticulousness I used in my previous career as an accountant was perfect for documenting the restoration steps.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the mess of paste and leather scraps, which I found quite repulsive at first.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried I would accidentally destroy a rare edition.
texts.B:
### Elena, The Community Workshop Leader
Elena teaches basic repair at a local library. [49]The project made me realize that we are living in a 'throwaway' culture and that people are desperate to learn how to preserve things.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have started the classes if the library manager hadn't noticed me repairing my own books in the reading room.[/56] [54]I was moved by the stories people shared about the books they brought in, often their only link to deceased relatives.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the physical exhaustion of speaking and demonstrating for six hours straight to be so draining.[/47] [55]I found the initial task of explaining the difference between 'repair' and 'restoration' surprisingly difficult for students to grasp.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that nobody would be interested in such an old-fashioned craft.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or social impact of the restoration environment or task?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) mentions the shock of silence/isolation, Sarah (B) the impact on her eyesight, and Elena (D) the exhaustion of teaching.

questions.question: notes an unexpected personal improvement in a skill or mental state?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) improved his manual dexterity, and Victor (C) found his ability to focus reached an all-time high.

questions.question: expresses a change in their outlook regarding artistic interpretation or modern culture?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) reflects on the cover as an interpretation, and Elena (D) on our 'throwaway' culture.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or skills were advantageous to the craft?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) used his chemistry background, and Victor (C) used his accountant's meticulousness.

questions.question: describes a challenge related to a painstaking process or sourcing specific materials?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) mentions de-acidifying pages, and Victor (C) mentions the scarcity of authentic materials.

questions.question: admits to having had prior doubts or having formed an unexpected emotional connection to a specific item?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) felt a connection to a client's diary, and Victor (C) worried about destroying a rare edition.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about their role or the goal of restoration?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) saw himself as an invisible caretaker, and Victor (C) realized it's about stability, not looking new.

questions.question: highlights the value of the guidance received or the personal stories shared by others?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) valued her mentor's wisdom, and Elena (D) was moved by the students' personal stories.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the mess or the complexity of definitions?
questions.explanation: Victor (C) adjusted to the mess of paste/scraps, and Elena (D) found explaining definitions difficult.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external factor or event that led to their involvement?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) took a course at a craft fair, and Elena (D) was noticed by a library manager.
